title: My First Post Ever
excerpt: I have absolute no idea why I'm doing this.
content:
I never had a blog before so be welcome to my first one and also to my first post ever (~maybe ever)! 

 Here I will try to write about random stuff when the time allows me to. I hope you enjoy it and find it interesting, or not actually. Probably a lot of contradiction will happen but I will try to be as honest as possible to my present self when writting not considering my opinions from the past. I will also share some of my project ideas, and half ways there. 

 Since I don't want to use a database for this it will be most likely hardcoded as json files. I might change it in the future if a heartless bad guy gets access to this humble website. So far, so good (first day only and counting). Furthemore, I would like to inform to you scumbag scraping bot that you are not welcome here. 

 Thanks for reading whatever I write, you are a good friend!

title: Mushrooms and Capitalism
excerpt: What fungi networks can teach us about markets, value, and decay.
content:
DRAFT — scaffold, to be fleshed out. 

 An intro hooking the reader: the moment I started thinking about mushrooms as more than dinner. 

 Outline to cover: 

 - Mycelium as a decentralised network, and the lazy comparison to 'the market' (and why it might be wrong). 

 - Foraging, commodification, and what happens when a wild thing gets a price tag. 

 - Growth for growth's sake vs. decomposition — fungi only thrive by breaking things down. A degrowth metaphor that probably gets away from me. 

 - Closing thought: what we lose when we measure everything in yield.

title: Ausangate
excerpt: Notes from the trail around Peru's rainbow mountain.
content:
First of all I would like to state here that this was the hardest hike I did so far in my life but also the coolest. As soon as I came back from Peru and people figured out I went to Cusco absolutely everyone asked me the question ~how was macchu picchu???~ To be honest everytime I heard that question I got hurt like if I was a nail and the question was like a huge hammer coming to stamp one single thing to rotulate a whole vast and diverse culture. Peru is simply amazing, people, landscapes, food and any other subject you can imagine. When in Cusco, since I didn't plan anything beforehand I needed to make a decision: macchu picchu or ausengate. Well, if you have a descent amount of neurons you know my decision. 

After making my decision for Ausengate I started to stress the internet and travel agencies (which I'm most of the time against) looking for something afordable. It was impossible. Based on the challange level of the hike, the amount of days (4 to 5) and the equipment the prices are basically InSaNe! Hopefully I found somewhere the number of a local guide that actually lives together with his whole family right there! Cirilo is his name! After a few messages he told me he would be around Cusco next day and we agreed to talk. The price was fair: 900 soles. Basically 25% of the cheapest price I had found so far and all included (tent, food, water). Besides, all the money stays with the community in Ausengate - no middle man! Ahh, one important detail, since I was alone I was also looking for a group to join since private guidance was out of question. Cirilo told me there was two italian dudes that maybe wouldn't mind if join and they didn't in the end. The Francescos! Both from Milan. I can say we are good friends now and the craziest thing is that both could speak portuguese since they learned it in their capoeira practices. 

 Afteer settled the deal I departed next morning together with the Francescos and 3 other people from Belgium that would split to go to the rainbow mountains in the second day. We drove a long way to Tinki and from there up to the starting point: Cirilo's house! His wife received us with a simple but nice breakfast. From there we hiked to Tinki, all the way facing the crazy mountains that were getting closer and closer. The terrain was humid and unique, high altitude, flat and hilly, green with some water spots and so many alpacas. The day was beautiful when we reached upis where we stayed for the night. The belgiums splitted in the beggining since they were doing only 2 or 3 days and finishing halfway in the rainbow mountains. So they were in the 7 lakes in the morning and we meet in upis in the afternoon. second day we starting the hike passing thorough paso de arapa, and passo de de apacheta and finally we split, I guess in ananta. All the way such a beautiful rocky terrain with some interesting weird fauna and flora with hot springs. Pumacocha lake from a top perspective while taking a break is something I will never forget. Me and the Francescos we kept hiking and we passed through the paradiseish like ausangate pass and its stunning ausengate lake. This was one of the most beautiful days of my life like something I have never seen before in a perfect weather. We rested in a camp site in the beginning of the route to palomani pass. It rained that night quite a lot. I was told Palomani pass is the highest pass of the trail, so I started to use as much coca leafs as possible in the day before already in all possible ways to use it (tea, candy, leafs both chewing and leaving it in the gum). This desperate action was due to the hard altitude hit I got in the first day. Luckily the belgium crew was composed by two doctors holding ibuprofene and other drugs for altitude that they kindly gave it to me. I sleept like a baby after that. Anyways, that night I was very focused on the rest for the highest pass next day which was around 5.2 thousand meters! 

 I confess was affraid that day but of course as a regular average everyday man I didn't share it with nobody. The route was very steep since the beggining. It was really hard but gorgeous. When we finally reached the passage I almost cry - the highest place I have been in my entire life and it was beautiful. It looked exactly like the rainbow mountain and all its layers but with no one. We chilled there and everyone was happy. Here is the photos with the Francescos and the two from the crew (the guide and the caballero). The horses were amazing tho, increadible indurance for the mountains, probably a breed that adapted to the altitude and maybe could be the reason why they were quite small. We walk all the way back of the Ausengate mountain that day, increadible landscape and a huge contrast between the deep dark rocks, blue lake, white snow and rainbow colors in the opposite side. We hiked a lot that day, like a lot. The tuffest day for sure. After the pass you go flat then down like a valley and then you need to go all the way up again passing the 5000 meters in jampa pass. Massive effort but we all did it with a few breaks and even a 15 min nap time during the route. However, the weather got us as soon we got in the highest spot. I can't even describe what actually happened. Insane wind, ice and snow at the same time. I have been in snowstorms before but nothing like that. It was snow, ice, harsh wind and fog at the same time. I couldn't see even 5 meters in front of me. We proceeded carefully and we didn't dare to stay there to see one of the iconics views from the path, a lagoon view between the red mountains that I can't even remember based on the fear at that point. Unfortunatelly we missed that in the sake of safety. Overcomed that weather we started descending for the last camp site almost inside the west part of the Ausengate mountain with some ancient buildings from a previous village I assume. From there the sky at night was so pretty and all around us was inreadible mountains. It looked like if we were being wrapped by it when spinning the eyes around the tents. 

 Last day up we honestly just enjoyed since the hardest part were behind us. 7 lakes that's how they called. It is so pretty but also very touristic. We were lucky we were there very early morning since we camped there and it was so funny to see the people coming while we were returning. Once reaching the last spot of the hike we just chilled and ate something. Also we played some chess and I beated both Italians more than once probably per pure luck. The crew were amazing and very friendly. I hope they are well. They told us that they work in the trail almost every week with two or three days of resting between each new start. Crazy and tuff life but also simply beautiful. I wounder how many times they surrounded already that mountain. I headed back to Tinki after a few hours and then we got a intercity public bus back to Cusco. Sad and happy that it was over, hard to explain. I hope I never forget those memories. This is what I got from Ausengate: beautiful memories fullfilled with landscapes, alpacas aquaflorida and coca, respect for the mountains and two random italian friends that speak portuguese and practice capoeira. If you are reading this thank you guys for the fellowship, jokes around and for sharing your coca leaves. Here me out, I still want to go one day to Macchu Picchu and it is probably increadible but I think choosing the Ausengate this time was my best trip decision. Maybe even from all trips I did until now. 

 I extremely recommend Cirilo and his crew as guide for Ausengate. Here is his phone/whatsapp: +51 941 005 350

title: Why Is It So Hard to Take Business Actions?
excerpt: A graveyard of business ideas I never started, and what keeps me from pulling the trigger.
content:
DRAFT — scaffold, to be fleshed out. 

 The honest question behind this post: why is the gap between 'I have an idea' and 'I started something' so wide? Fear, time, money, comfort, perfectionism — name the real culprit. 

 The graveyard of ideas (so far): 

 - An amethyst web shop — sourcing crystals and selling them online. 

 - A startup in the energy market. 

 - A software and hardware company with my roommate and a friend from Brazil. 

 - A software inspection / codebase-audit company for other companies. 

 - A South American bakery in Copenhagen, selling pan de bono and pão de queijo. 

 Outline to cover: 

 - For each idea: the spark, why it excited me, and the exact moment I stalled. 

 - The pattern across all of them — what they have in common in how they died. 

 - What 'taking action' would actually look like this time, in small, scary, concrete steps. 

 - A promise to my future self (and a date to check back on it).
